0: <title>Life cycle assessment of biodiesel production from beef tallow in Brazil </title>
1 Vitor M. Z. Sousa . Sandra M. Luz 1 Armando Caldeira-Pires Frederico S. Machado 3 Cristiano M. Silveira 3
Received: 30 December 2015 Accepted: 11 September 2017 / Published online: 22 September 2017 Springer-Verlag GmbH Germany 2017
1: Purpose Renewable energy sources, particularly biofuels, are being promoted as possible solutions to address global warming and the depletion of petroleum resources. In this context, biodiesel is a solution to the growing demand for renewable fuels. Beef tallow is the second leading raw mate- rial after soybean oil used in biodiesel production in Brazil. Evaluating and addressing the environmental impacts of beef tallow biodiesel are of great importance for its life cycle im- pact assessment (LCIA).

Methods Inventory data on tallow and biodiesel production were collected from the literature and from a primary data source provided by a Brazilian biodiesel plant. The modeled system represents the Brazilian reality for the 2005-2015 de- cade. Subsequently, the environmental impacts of beef tallow biodiesel production were characterized for a selection of en- vironmental impact indicators: global warming potential (GWP), acidification potential (AP), eutrophication potential (EP), and water footprint (assessed based on blue water use (BWU) and blue water consumption (BWC) indicators). From

<list>1 Gama Campus, University of Brasilia, Brasília, DF, CEP 72444-240, Brazil 
2 Mechanical Engineering Department, University of Brasilia, Darcy Ribeiro Campus, Brasília, DF, CEP 70910-900, Brazil 
3 Research and Development Center Leopoldo Américo Miguez de Mello - CENPES, Petróleo Brasileiro S.A. - PETROBRAS, Rio de Janeiro, RJ, Brazil</list>

the characterization of these environmental burdens, the main sources of environmental impact were evaluated. Sensitivity analysis was conducted to verify the influence of key parameters (emission factor, energy consumption, and prices) on changes in the environmental load of beef tallow biodiesel.

Results and discussion Carbon flux results indicate that beef tallow biodiesel production acts as a carbon source. Namely, pasture carbon uptake (91% of all carbon input) is lower than combined biogenic and fossil CO2 emissions, which are con- trolled by cattle enteric fermentation as methane (72%) and by thermal energy processes (25%). Otherwise, thermal energy production accounts for 80% of total AP emissions, and cattle urine and manure are responsible for 70% of total EP emis- sions. The BWC and BWU water footprints of the whole process are controlled by electricity usage, which was greater than 90% for each indicator due to the high proportion of total energy (70%) derived from hydropower in Brazil. The envi- ronmental burden from transportation is minimal compared to other processes. Tallow biodiesel GWP can be improved if the carbon uptake potential from grass and low fertilizer utiliza- tion are accurately considered, as observed in the sensitivity analysis. For each MJ of beef tallow biodiesel produced, 4.6 g of CO2 is released to the atmosphere.

Conclusions Methane emissions, mainly due to cattle enteric fermentation, and thermal energy processes at the industrial units were the main sources of environmental GWP, AP, and EP impacts. Otherwise, water footprint indicators were asso- ciated with the high proportion of total energy derived from hydropower in Brazil.
Keywords Beeftallow . Biodiesel . Cattle management Environmental impacts . Life cycle assessment

5: The life cycle assessment (LCA) of biofuel production re- quires a country-specific approach when assessing the envi- ronmental impact potential (Amores et al. 2013; Panichelli et al. 2009). LCA studies on second-generation biofuels have usually focused on bioethanol and biodiesel because these fuels are substitutes for blends containing gasoline and fossil diesel, respectively (González-Garcia et al. 2013; Rodrigues et al. 2014). Thus, several publications have presented envi- ronmental evaluations of biodiesel production from vegetable waste, vegetable oils, and animal fats in different countries and conditions (Cho et al. 2013; Dufour and Iribarren 2012; Malça et al. 2014; Panichelli et al. 2009). Further efforts are needed to improve data access and inventory data display for biofuels, especially those fuels with various raw materials of both vegetal and animal origin.

In this context, the biodiesel industry in Brazil is expanding. Several raw materials are used in biodiesel pro- duction, including 75% from soybean oil and 17% from beef tallow (Brasil 2013). Beef production in Brazil is an agricul- tural activity that has grown substantially over the past years, and is now well established (Brasil 2013). Today, Brazil is one of the largest beef exporters in the world (Makiya and Fraisse 2015), with 34.4 million head of cattle slaughtered in 2013 (IBGE E 2013). It is estimated that over 800 million kilograms of beef fat per year is produced in the country (Brasil 2013).

The tallow production system has multiple outputs of com- mercial interest, such as meat, leather, tallow, and edible offal. Tallow appears in this context as a by-product of beef process- ing (Bueno et al. 2012). Beef production is largely carried out with extensive management, starting with free cattle in pasture (Martins et al. 2011). The remains of slaughtered cattle such as bones, entrails, blood, and beef trimmings are turned into tal- low. Currently, tallow is used in the soap and lubricant indus- tries and as a raw material in the production of biodiesel. However, when the soap market is overloaded, tallow is usu- ally incinerated or disposed of in sanitary landfills, causing environmental impacts in both cases. Tallow utilization as a raw material for biodiesel production is intended to eliminate these sources of environmental impacts (Cunha et al. 2009). One of the great advantages of tallow over high-quality virgin vegetable oils is that tallow production is low cost, while high- quality virgin oils are more expensive to produce (Bhatti et al. 2008). Assessment of the potential environmental impacts of beef tallow production is important to support other studies or companies that seek to verify and improve the production process, as this raw material is produced in high volume and increasingly used for biodiesel production.

Within this framework, the present study reviews the existing literature in the fields of beef tallow and biodiesel production with the aim of providing reference Brazilian in- ventory data on beef tallow biodiesel. Furthermore, this work

also addresses the life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) of biodiesel and beef tallow production systems. Therefore, an LCA study was conducted to characterize the environmental profile of the beef tallow biodiesel system, considering that this source has been increasing in Brazil.

9: The goal of this study was to carry out an LCIA of a biodiesel production system for beef tallow to identify the gaps in beef tallow biodiesel production and address possible improve- ments in the production chain. With this purpose, an LCA study was conducted using foreground data based on the available literature in this field. The system was defined from a cradle-to-gate perspective. The functional unit of the study was 1 MJ of biodiesel at the gate of the biodiesel plant. The purpose of this system is to provide biodiesel that can be blended with diesel from a fossil source. Figure 1 shows a general description of the processes involved and the system boundaries considered. In this case, the system boundaries included cattle management, animal slaughter, rendering op- erations, and alkali-catalyzed transesterification, along with the required transportation and waste management when this information was available. It was decided to work with a sys- tem that has well-defined boundaries and not to expand them to cover the production of inputs and other products used. Instead, auxiliary processes from the GaBi 6.3 database (http://www.gabi-software.com/international/index/) were used (Thinkstep 2016). These processes are important for LCA studies and refer to processes that indirectly affect the overall account, e.g., electricity production, thermal energy production, water supply, and chemical production. The data cut-off criteria excluded from this LCA study included ma- chinery production, capital, producer and investment goods, infrastructure installation, and construction of the all produc- tion facilities.

11: <header>2.2 Key parameters, assumptions, and data quality </header>

The LCA was performed according to the ISO standards on LCA (ISO 2006a, b). The process for modeling the production of biodiesel from tallow was facilitated by GaBi 6.3R soft- ware. The LCIA method used was CML 2001 (April 2013), which evaluated the following indicators available in the GaBi 6.3R database: global warming potential (GWP), eutrophica- tion potential (EP), and acidification potential (AP). CML is one of the most widely used impact assessment systems with- in the LCA community and can be used in the main commer- cially available LCA software. The most commonly used LCA databases, GaBiR and ecoinvent®, have their flows
Fig. 1 General beef tallow biodiesel production flowchart

characterized with CML factors. Additionally, the water foot- print of tallow biodiesel production was assessed using blue water consumption (BWC) and blue water use (BWU) as indicators. These indicators are specific to GaBi® software, as described by Koehler and Thylmann (2012).

On the one hand, key data for modeling the raw material phase (cattle management, slaughter, and tallow production) were derived from the current literature on tallow production, always representing the real Brazilian system. The system description included beef tallow-based biodiesel production in Brazil over the past 10 years (2005-2015). Some data were collected from the GaBi 6.3R database when no information was available in the literature. Additionally, industrial process data were retrieved from primary data from a Brazilian bio- diesel plant.

All relevant data on biodiesel production were included except for data on alkali catalyst production. The catalyst was excluded from the inventory due to marginal input values and therefore minimal relevance to the overall result. All other chemical production and transportation data were accounted for when available. When not available but needed, secondary data from the GaBi 6.3R database and the current literature were used to complement the information.

Transportation distances were accounted for in major pro- cesses such as tallow transportation from the rendering plant to the biodiesel plant and fertilizer transportation to the field. Both transportation processes were modeled with a default distance of 100 km. Trucks with 40% thermal efficiency, 27- ton cargo capacity, and 85% cargo capacity usage as described in the GaBi 6.3 database were considered. All transportation inputs were considered with a default distance of 100 km and the same parameters as for tallow transportation. This distance was the average of all transportation input distances.
The cattle management system evaluated in this study is extensive (cattle free in pastures, an average of 1.5 animals/

ha), also incorporating the use of fertilizers in soil and land preparation (Brasil 2013). In this case, land use changes were not considered, as grazing has been established on the site for more than 30 years with no requirement for accounting. The only products considered to leave the system were meat and tallow, and the rest (leather, edible offal, blood, and condemned parts) were considered waste for simplification purposes.

As tallow production is a system with multiple outputs (meat and tallow), economic allocation was chosen mainly because meat is a product with a high market value and tallow has a comparatively much lower price. Otherwise, if mass allocation (as recommended by the ISO standards) was used, tallow would receive an environmental burden that is incon- sistent with its relative importance in the meat supply chain, although it is responsible for high water consumption in this supply chain.

Moreover, biodiesel production is also a process with mul- tiple outputs (biodiesel and glycerin); in this case, the environ- mental impacts must be correctly allocated to the outputs (Amores et al. 2013). Although the ISO standard recommends subdividing the system or performing system expansion in- stead of allocation, energy allocation was chosen since both glycerin and biodiesel are energy carriers.

The system description included beef tallow-based biodiesel production in Brazil over the past 10 years (2005-2015). Some data were collected from the GaBi 6.3R database when no information was available in the literature. Additionally, industrial process data were retrieved from primary data from a Brazilian bio- diesel plant.

All relevant data on biodiesel production were included except for data on alkali catalyst production. The catalyst was excluded from the inventory due to marginal input values and therefore minimal relevance to the overall result. All other chemical production and transportation data were accounted for when available. When not available but needed, secondary data from the GaBi 6.3R database and the current literature were used to complement the information.

Transportation distances were accounted for in major pro- cesses such as tallow transportation from the rendering plant to the biodiesel plant and fertilizer transportation to the field. Both transportation processes were modeled with a default distance of 100 km. Trucks with 40% thermal efficiency, 27- ton cargo capacity, and 85% cargo capacity usage as described in the GaBi 6.3 database were considered. All transportation inputs were considered with a default distance of 100 km and the same parameters as for tallow transportation. This distance was the average of all transportation input distances.
The cattle management system evaluated in this study is extensive (cattle free in pastures, an average of 1.5 animals/

ha), also incorporating the use of fertilizers in soil and land preparation (Brasil 2013). In this case, land use changes were not considered, as grazing has been established on the site for more than 30 years with no requirement for accounting. The only products considered to leave the system were meat and tallow, and the rest (leather, edible offal, blood, and condemned parts) were considered waste for simplification purposes.

As tallow production is a system with multiple outputs (meat and tallow), economic allocation was chosen mainly because meat is a product with a high market value and tallow has a comparatively much lower price. Otherwise, if mass allocation (as recommended by the ISO standards) was used, tallow would receive an environmental burden that is incon- sistent with its relative importance in the meat supply chain, although it is responsible for high water consumption in this supply chain.

Moreover, biodiesel production is also a process with mul- tiple outputs (biodiesel and glycerin); in this case, the environ- mental impacts must be correctly allocated to the outputs (Amores et al. 2013). Although the ISO standard recommends subdividing the system or performing system expansion in- stead of allocation, energy allocation was chosen since both glycerin and biodiesel are energy carriers.
13: <header>2.2 Key parameters, assumptions, and data quality </header>

The LCA was performed according to the ISO standards on LCA (ISO 2006a, b). The process for modeling the production of biodiesel from tallow was facilitated by GaBi 6.3R soft- ware. The LCIA method used was CML 2001 (April 2013), which evaluated the following indicators available in the GaBi 6.3R database: global warming potential (GWP), eutrophica- tion potential (EP), and acidification potential (AP). CML is one of the most widely used impact assessment systems with- in the LCA community and can be used in the main commer- cially available LCA software. The most commonly used LCA databases, GaBiR and ecoinvent®, have their flows
Fig. 1 General beef tallow biodiesel production flowchart

characterized with CML factors. Additionally, the water foot- print of tallow biodiesel production was assessed using blue water consumption (BWC) and blue water use (BWU) as indicators. These indicators are specific to GaBi® software, as described by Koehler and Thylmann (2012).

15: Beef tallow biodiesel production comprises four main processes:

<list>1. Cattle management: includes cattle management from the pasture until slaughter; 
2. Slaughter: cattle slaughter stage, including the removal of entrails; beef production; and rendering feedstock, bones, and blood as products;</list>

<list>3. Tallow production: the use of disposed bones, blood, fat, and meat pieces for tallow production; 
4. Biodiesel production from tallow: the pretreatment of crude tallow oil and the transesterification to biodiesel.</list>

The slaughter and tallow production processes are de- scribed in the environmental technical guidelines of the Environmental Sanitation Technology Company (CETESB) (Pacheco 2006; Pacheco and Yamanaka 2006).
16: In the management phase, cattle live, for the most part, free in pastures. Sodium chloride and calcium phosphate are provid- ed freely to cattle. A few days before slaughter, feed is given to the livestock as a supplement that consists of 78.7% corn, 20% soybean meal, 15% ammonium sulfate, 1% urea, and 0.2% calcium carbonate (Corrêa et al. 2000), and the production of this feed was considered in the LCA study. Cattle are ready for slaughter in 3 years when they reach a liveweight of 450 kg.
17: The slaughterhouse is the location of slaughter and pro- cessing of animal products, as described in Brazilian legislation (Brasil 1952). The slaughter process begins with the transport of animals to the slaughterhouse, mostly in specific trucks. Upon arriving at the slaugh- terhouse, livestock are separated into lots according to their origin and remain in corrals, fasting between 16 and 24 h. This is necessary due to the high-stress live- stock are subjected to before being slaughtered. This time is required for the animals to rest SO that there is an improvement in meat quality. Analysis of the effluent from washing cattle trucks and washing the animals was not included in this study due to the lack of information on water consumption at every stage.

The next step involves animal death and carcass pro- cessing, namely, leather removal, evisceration, meat strip- ping, and carcass boning. Carcass processing continues in the evisceration step, which includes the removal of edi- ble and inedible offal. All entrails undergo inspection, and if deemed appropriate, they are transported to the process- ing plant; otherwise, they are destined to rendering plants for the production of flour and/or tallow. After removal, the carcass is ready to be stripped and stored. The car- casses are forwarded to cold rooms and then carried to butchers or processed inside the slaughterhouse if it has a butcher section for stripping. After these steps, accord- ing to Pacheco and Yamanaka (2006), 39% of the liveweight becomes meat, 38% is used for rendering feed- stock, 9% is used for leather, 5% is used for edible offal,
3% constitutes blood, and the rest (7%) constitutes condemned parts.

Rendering plants are industries that use by-products derived from the production of beef to produce flour or tallow (Rebouças et al. 2010). The rendering plants are usually at- tached to the slaughterhouses and process, in addition to the by-products of beef production, the by-products of poultry meat production, fish, and butcher scraps. According to Pacheco (2006), 7% of the total liveweight is converted into tallow at the gate of the plant.

The raw material is received by the rendering plant, where it is stored or sent for quick processing. All materials are milled and triturated, generating a mass that is transported to the cooking step. Cooking is one of the main operations of the rendering plant. The cooking step can occur following three different pathways: wet, dry, or drying. Wet milling consists of the direct application of steam to the material, which facilitates the separation of the solid phase, tallow, and water. The dry method consists of indirectly heating the material to eliminate the moisture. Drying is similar to the dry process, except for the use of atomizers in drying the remaining vapors. In the cooking stage, equipment such as digesters and autoclaves are used at high temperatures and pressures for between 1 and h.

The next step in cooking is percolation. The cooked mate- rial is directed to a percolation pan and heated to steam, where the tallow is separated from the solids in suspension. Then, the material proceeds to tallow purification. The tallow is centri- fuged, filtered, and held in a decanter tank for storage and eventual separation of the aqueous phase. From the tank, the tallow is removed and placed in trucks for transport. After this process, the raw tallow is sent to the biodiesel production unit.

22: Raw tallow from rendering plants arrives at the facility in specialized trucks. Tallow has high acidity (3.5% maximum) and phosphorus levels (6.5% maximum) due to its composi- tion. These two factors determine the type of treatment that the tallow will undergo to obtain refined tallow oil. The treatment for reducing acidity consists of free fatty acid separation. For phosphorus separation, phosphoric acid is used as a reactant. For tallow containing low amounts of phosphorus, physical treatment, which has 95% efficiency, is suitable. Tallow is treated to obtain refined tallow oil, which then enters the transesterification process.

Transesterification is a chemical process involving a reac- tion between methanol and refined tallow oil aided by a cata- lyst, in this case, sodium methylate in methanol that produces the biodiesel. In this plant, there are two transesterification reactors in which methanol, sodium methylate (catalyst), and

refined tallow oil are mixed in-line and cooled in heat ex- changers. The methanol is recycled, the biodiesel is washed until it reaches neutral pH, and the glycerin is treated and stored to be sold on the market. The final effluents are treated, refined, and reused in the process again.
23: Table 1 lists the main inventory data for tallow production from beef, constituting a key outcome of this study. The in- ventory data show the most important flows in the process and indicate which flow is related to each impact category. Biodiesel and tallow production processes were modeled based on a generic model, which could be used to model other cases such as dairy cattle and other biodiesel plants that handle different pretreatment processes for crude oil.

In Table 1, all relevant flows that enter and leave the pro- cesses are included. Tallow production requires considerable energy, both electrical and thermal. Electricity is required for all processes in tallow production from cattle management in the pasture to the rendering plant operation, and thermal en- ergy is used to render the feedstock.

Beef food rations have a high input value because they are provided to cattle 80 days before slaughter to improve the beef quality. For simplification purposes, all rations consumed by cattle were assumed to have been transformed into manure. Some fertilizers are used to improve pasture quality. For ex- ample, nitrogen/potassium and triple superphosphate fertil- izers are used for this purpose in small amounts. Brazilian pastures are composed mostly of grass plants (e.g., Brachiaria spp.) that absorb nitrogen in several ways, includ- ing biological nitrogen fixation (BNF) from organic matter left in the soil and from fertilizers (Costa et al. 2006). BNF repre- sents the smaller component of nitrogen fixation in pastures (Wagner 2011). Nitrogen is mostly absorbed directly from urine and manure that cattle deposit on the soil (Costa et al. 2006). Therefore, there is no need for high fertilizer applica- tion in pastures. The pasture nitrogen cycle is also evaluated in the model.

As observed in the tallow production outputs in Table 1, significant emissions exist from cattle management, such as ammonia, methane, and nitrous oxide, and the significant residues left after the slaughter process. Ammonia emissions are high because it easily volatilizes from cattle urine. Nitrous oxide emissions are from enteric fermentation and from manure on the field. Methane emissions are high in cattle management, with enteric fermentation accounting for most of these emissions. Cederberg et al. (2009) studied the meth- ane emissions from cattle and found that a 3-year-old produces approximately 60 kg of CH4 per year in Brazil. If we consider the methane emissions from the Brazilian cattle herd, the total amount produced becomes the largest GWP source in biodiesel production from beef tallow.

In the cattle management system, an input water flow is needed for tallow production in the cooking step to transform the rendering feedstock into raw tallow. In this case, all input water was considered as a vapor output for simplification pur- poses. In biodiesel production, water is needed for the produc- tion of certain auxiliary materials such as methanol, sodium hydroxide, and hydrochloric acid.

Methane emissions are high in cattle management, with enteric fermentation accounting for most of these emissions. Cederberg et al. (2009) studied the meth- ane emissions from cattle and found that a 3-year-old produces approximately 60 kg of CH4 per year in Brazil. If we consider the methane emissions from the Brazilian cattle herd, the total amount produced becomes the largest GWP source in biodiesel production from beef tallow.

In the cattle management system, an input water flow is needed for tallow production in the cooking step to transform the rendering feedstock into raw tallow. In this case, all input water was considered as a vapor output for simplification pur- poses. In biodiesel production, water is needed for the produc- tion of certain auxiliary materials such as methanol, sodium hydroxide, and hydrochloric acid.
25: Table 1 lists the main inventory data for tallow production from beef, constituting a key outcome of this study. The in- ventory data show the most important flows in the process and indicate which flow is related to each impact category. Biodiesel and tallow production processes were modeled based on a generic model, which could be used to model other cases such as dairy cattle and other biodiesel plants that handle different pretreatment processes for crude oil.

In Table 1, all relevant flows that enter and leave the pro- cesses are included. Tallow production requires considerable energy, both electrical and thermal. Electricity is required for all processes in tallow production from cattle management in the pasture to the rendering plant operation, and thermal en- ergy is used to render the feedstock.

Beef food rations have a high input value because they are provided to cattle 80 days before slaughter to improve the beef quality. For simplification purposes, all rations consumed by cattle were assumed to have been transformed into manure. Some fertilizers are used to improve pasture quality. For ex- ample, nitrogen/potassium and triple superphosphate fertil- izers are used for this purpose in small amounts. Brazilian pastures are composed mostly of grass plants (e.g., Brachiaria spp.) that absorb nitrogen in several ways, includ- ing biological nitrogen fixation (BNF) from organic matter left in the soil and from fertilizers (Costa et al. 2006). BNF repre- sents the smaller component of nitrogen fixation in pastures (Wagner 2011). Nitrogen is mostly absorbed directly from urine and manure that cattle deposit on the soil (Costa et al. 2006). Therefore, there is no need for high fertilizer applica- tion in pastures. The pasture nitrogen cycle is also evaluated in the model.

As observed in the tallow production outputs in Table 1, significant emissions exist from cattle management, such as ammonia, methane, and nitrous oxide, and the significant residues left after the slaughter process. Ammonia emissions are high because it easily volatilizes from cattle urine. Nitrous oxide emissions are from enteric fermentation and from manure on the field. Methane emissions are high in cattle management, with enteric fermentation accounting for most of these emissions. Cederberg et al. (2009) studied the meth- ane emissions from cattle and found that a 3-year-old produces approximately 60 kg of CH4 per year in Brazil. If we consider the methane emissions from the Brazilian cattle herd, the total amount produced becomes the largest GWP source in biodiesel production from beef tallow.

In the cattle management system, an input water flow is needed for tallow production in the cooking step to transform the rendering feedstock into raw tallow. In this case, all input water was considered as a vapor output for simplification pur- poses. In biodiesel production, water is needed for the produc- tion of certain auxiliary materials such as methanol, sodium hydroxide, and hydrochloric acid.
26: <header>3.1 LCIA of biodiesel production from beef tallow </header>

Table 2 shows the results for three impact categories (GWP, AP, and EP) and the water footprint (assessed based on BWU and BWC indicators) of tallow and bio- diesel production and processing. In general, tallow pro- duction (upstream agricultural processes, slaughter, and rendering operations) had a higher environmental impact than biodiesel production (biodiesel plant site) even after considering the CO2 uptake by pasture and animal feed production. A major reason for this result is that there is strict control over the biodiesel industry regarding the waste of materials (e.g., methanol and catalyst) and pro- cess losses (effluent treatment). However, in the cattle management phase, it is impossible to control emissions to the atmosphere, SO they contribute strongly to the GWP and in small proportions to the AP and EP.

The GWP includes both biogenic and fossil carbon. GWP negative values account for the carbon uptake in the form of two main biogenic carbon flows, pasture materials as well as corn and soybeans, to support cattle throughout their lifetime. Moreover, biogenic carbon is also emitted due to the biodiesel composition of Brazilian diesel. Regarding thermal energy, high AP values are associated with the heavy fuel oil burned and its high sulfur content (1%). In Table 2, all relative con- tributions from each process are calculated based on the total tallow + biodiesel production values for each impact category. The carbon uptake by pasture and animal feed production processes are carbon sinks, whereas CO2 is absorbed by both pasture and livestock rations, specifically corn and soybean.

Biodiesel production has no major source of environmental impact compared to beef tallow production. All wastes and effluents leaving the process undergo treatment before dispos- al or reuse. Thus, the AP, EP, and GWP are lower than those for beef tallow production. The main cause of GWP emissions is the burning of heavy fuel oil to heat reactors, which occurs during the transesterification reaction.

Baitz et al. (2013) describe water consumption as water losses at the watershed level, which are caused by natural events such as evaporation, water integration into products, and human activities. This water consumption is associated with losses during water use. Degradative water use is

associated with quality alterations; for instance, river water is transformed into cooling water after use. "Blue water" is as- sociated with surface and groundwater, excluding rainwater. The BWU and BWC can both be understood as blue water withdrawal, and BWU also involves the blue water released from the "technosphere." Hence, regarding the water foot- print, tallow production contributed almost completely to the BWC and BWU indicators at 95 and 91%, respectively.

Electricity production was primarily responsible for the BWU indicator, since hydroelectricity is responsible for 70% of Brazilian electricity. Since there is no degradative use of water in electricity production, the BWU indicator is high for electricity production. Animal feed production is mainly re- sponsible for the BWC indicator, since large quantities of water are consumed during ration production (crop irrigation). In BWC, water is retrieved from rivers, lakes, or groundwater

29: <header>3.1 LCIA of biodiesel production from beef tallow </header>

Table 2 shows the results for three impact categories (GWP, AP, and EP) and the water footprint (assessed based on BWU and BWC indicators) of tallow and bio- diesel production and processing. In general, tallow pro- duction (upstream agricultural processes, slaughter, and rendering operations) had a higher environmental impact than biodiesel production (biodiesel plant site) even after considering the CO2 uptake by pasture and animal feed production. A major reason for this result is that there is strict control over the biodiesel industry regarding the waste of materials (e.g., methanol and catalyst) and pro- cess losses (effluent treatment). However, in the cattle management phase, it is impossible to control emissions to the atmosphere, SO they contribute strongly to the GWP and in small proportions to the AP and EP.

The GWP includes both biogenic and fossil carbon. GWP negative values account for the carbon uptake in the form of two main biogenic carbon flows, pasture materials as well as corn and soybeans, to support cattle throughout their lifetime. Moreover, biogenic carbon is also emitted due to the biodiesel composition of Brazilian diesel. Regarding thermal energy, high AP values are associated with the heavy fuel oil burned and its high sulfur content (1%). In Table 2, all relative con- tributions from each process are calculated based on the total tallow + biodiesel production values for each impact category. The carbon uptake by pasture and animal feed production processes are carbon sinks, whereas CO2 is absorbed by both pasture and livestock rations, specifically corn and soybean.

Biodiesel production has no major source of environmental impact compared to beef tallow production. All wastes and effluents leaving the process undergo treatment before dispos- al or reuse. Thus, the AP, EP, and GWP are lower than those for beef tallow production. The main cause of GWP emissions is the burning of heavy fuel oil to heat reactors, which occurs during the transesterification reaction.

associated with quality alterations; for instance, river water is transformed into cooling water after use. "Blue water" is as- sociated with surface and groundwater, excluding rainwater. The BWU and BWC can both be understood as blue water withdrawal, and BWU also involves the blue water released from the "technosphere." Hence, regarding the water foot- print, tallow production contributed almost completely to the BWC and BWU indicators at 95 and 91%, respectively.

Electricity production was primarily responsible for the BWU indicator, since hydroelectricity is responsible for 70% of Brazilian electricity. Since there is no degradative use of water in electricity production, the BWU indicator is high for electricity production. Animal feed production is mainly re- sponsible for the BWC indicator, since large quantities of water are consumed during ration production (crop irrigation). In BWC, water is retrieved from rivers, lakes, or groundwater

and is used in crop irrigation, illustrating degradative use. Biodiesel production made small contributions to the BWC and BWU indicators of 5 and 9.3%, respectively, compared with tallow production. The BWC and BWU indicators in biodiesel production are related to electricity production and other auxiliary processes that use water. The biodiesel produc- tion contributions to the BWC and BWU indicators are lower than those of tallow production, mainly because upstream agricultural processes demand abundant water and because of the high electricity demand. Biodiesel production involves strict controls over water consumption and effluent, avoiding waste and reusing the inputs whenever possible.
33: <header>3.1 LCIA of biodiesel production from beef tallow </header>

Table 2 shows the results for three impact categories (GWP, AP, and EP) and the water footprint (assessed based on BWU and BWC indicators) of tallow and bio- diesel production and processing. In general, tallow pro- duction (upstream agricultural processes, slaughter, and rendering operations) had a higher environmental impact than biodiesel production (biodiesel plant site) even after considering the CO2 uptake by pasture and animal feed production. A major reason for this result is that there is strict control over the biodiesel industry regarding the waste of materials (e.g., methanol and catalyst) and pro- cess losses (effluent treatment). However, in the cattle management phase, it is impossible to control emissions to the atmosphere, SO they contribute strongly to the GWP and in small proportions to the AP and EP.

The GWP includes both biogenic and fossil carbon. GWP negative values account for the carbon uptake in the form of two main biogenic carbon flows, pasture materials as well as corn and soybeans, to support cattle throughout their lifetime. Moreover, biogenic carbon is also emitted due to the biodiesel composition of Brazilian diesel. Regarding thermal energy, high AP values are associated with the heavy fuel oil burned and its high sulfur content (1%). In Table 2, all relative con- tributions from each process are calculated based on the total tallow + biodiesel production values for each impact category. The carbon uptake by pasture and animal feed production processes are carbon sinks, whereas CO2 is absorbed by both pasture and livestock rations, specifically corn and soybean.

Biodiesel production has no major source of environmental impact compared to beef tallow production. All wastes and effluents leaving the process undergo treatment before dispos- al or reuse. Thus, the AP, EP, and GWP are lower than those for beef tallow production. The main cause of GWP emissions is the burning of heavy fuel oil to heat reactors, which occurs during the transesterification reaction.

Baitz et al. (2013) describe water consumption as water losses at the watershed level, which are caused by natural events such as evaporation, water integration into products, and human activities. This water consumption is associated with losses during water use. Degradative water use is

associated with quality alterations; for instance, river water is transformed into cooling water after use. "Blue water" is as- sociated with surface and groundwater, excluding rainwater. The BWU and BWC can both be understood as blue water withdrawal, and BWU also involves the blue water released from the "technosphere." Hence, regarding the water foot- print, tallow production contributed almost completely to the BWC and BWU indicators at 95 and 91%, respectively.

Electricity production was primarily responsible for the BWU indicator, since hydroelectricity is responsible for 70% of Brazilian electricity. Since there is no degradative use of water in electricity production, the BWU indicator is high for electricity production. Animal feed production is mainly re- sponsible for the BWC indicator, since large quantities of water are consumed during ration production (crop irrigation). In BWC, water is retrieved from rivers, lakes, or groundwater

and is used in crop irrigation, illustrating degradative use. Biodiesel production made small contributions to the BWC and BWU indicators of 5 and 9.3%, respectively, compared with tallow production. The BWC and BWU indicators in biodiesel production are related to electricity production and other auxiliary processes that use water. The biodiesel produc- tion contributions to the BWC and BWU indicators are lower than those of tallow production, mainly because upstream agricultural processes demand abundant water and because of the high electricity demand. Biodiesel production involves strict controls over water consumption and effluent, avoiding waste and reusing the inputs whenever possible.
36: <header>3.2 Identification of the main sources of environmental impacts </header>

The relative environmental impact results shown in Fig. 2 represent the contribution of each major process to the total emissions. Tallow transportation was accounted for because the modeled system included tallow transportation to the bio- diesel plant over a significant distance.

The AP and EP showed the same pattern (major contribu- tions to environmental impacts of beef tallow production), although a different pattern was identified for the GWP (bal- anced contributions to biodiesel and tallow production). In both cases, tallow transportation had a minimal contribution to all impact categories. Concerning diesel, low consumption based on distance was assumed. The AP associated with trans- portation was due to the diesel sulfur content, which was 1800 ppm S (currently, the sulfur contents of diesel are 10

and 500 ppm S), according to the National Petroleum Agency (ANP) resolution (ANP 2013). The EP is associated with NO emissions during diesel consumption. These gases can react with water to form nitrogenous compounds, thereby contributing to environmental eutrophication and increasing the amount of nutrients present in soils. Based on the evalua- tion of the GWP of biodiesel production, tallow production, and transportation, a different pattern can be observed in Fig. 2. Therefore, tallow production has a higher environmental impact potential than biodiesel production.

Figure 3 shows the AP, EP, and GWP contributions relative to each process of biodiesel production. The "Rest of processes" is defined as all other processes that do not have significant envi- ronmental burdens alone but become significant when consid- ered together. The AP is given in sulfur dioxide equivalents (SO2-Eq.) and is associated with the ability of a substance to release H+ ions and thereby cause a local pH decrease (Baitz et al. 2013). Acidification due to biodiesel production is mainly caused by fossil fuels with high sulfur contents. In electricity production, SOX emissions from diesel burning in thermoelectric plants contribute to the AP. Fossil fuel production has a high AP because of the diesel desulfurization process and the burning of fossil fuels in refineries. In addition to electricity production, heavy fuel oil is burned in boilers to generate sufficient thermal energy to heat the reactor where the transesterification reaction occurs. This heavy fuel oil contains approximately 1% by mass of sulfur. When the oil is burned, SOx is emitted and contributes to the AP.
The EP considers the enrichment of nutrients at a certain location and is highly associated with nitrogenous compounds,
Fig. 2 Relative contributions to the impact categories of the main processes in beef tallow biodiesel production

which can cause over-fertilization of soil and water. The EP is calculated in phosphate equivalents (PO4-Eq) (Baitz et al. 2013; Wang and Mu 2014). Emissions of nitrogenous compound are higher during the treatment of effluent, which contains hydrocar- bons and organic compounds. Hydrocarbons are released into the effluent during the biodiesel wash step to remove fatty acid esters. Other organic compounds are related to the effluent treat- ment process because they are added to improve the transesterification reaction. All other processes, mostly associat- ed with the gases emitted during diesel and heavy fuel oil burn- ing, contribute less than 10% to the EP.

The key greenhouse gases (GHGs) emitted from anthropo- genic activities are carbon dioxide and methane. These gases contribute to the earth's greenhouse effect, which results in a warming effect on the planet's surface. The GWP is calculated and presented in carbon dioxide equivalents (CO2-Eq.) (Baitz et al. 2013). During biodiesel production, the GWP is signif- icant during both thermal energy production and fossil fuel production. All heavy fuel oil burned in boilers turns into CO and CO2, which enter the atmosphere. Heavy fuel oil consumption is high to fulfill the plant's thermal energy needs. Fossil fuel production also contributed to GHG emissions. In
Fig. 3 Relative contributions to the AP, EP, and GWP of the biodiesel production processes

this process, the main source of the GWP is the CO2 emitted from the burning of hydrocarbons to refine fuels (in this case, diesel and heavy fuel oil). All impact potentials related to other processes ("rest of processes") are mainly due to the production of supplies used in the biodiesel plant.

Figure 4 shows the relative contributions to the AP and EP of each process in tallow production. Fertilizer production does not have a significant impact on either of the categories evaluated because fertilizer is used in very small amounts in the pasture, reducing its entire impact chain. Another reason for the low contribution of fertilizer production in both poten- tial impact categories is that all other processes have higher emission levels than the fertilizer production process, making their relative contributions very small. Animal feed produc- tion encompasses the production of rations, which are given to cattle days before slaughter. Rations are composed of corn, soybean, urea, ammonium sulfate, and limestone. Therefore, the EP originates from the fertilizer used on corn and soybean crops and from ammonium sulfate production because all ra- tions eventually become manure that is disposed of in the field. The AP and EP from electricity production represent almost nothing with respect to thermal energy and tallow pro- duction mainly because the amount of electricity used is small, thereby reducing its impact chain and increasing the impacts associated with other processes such as thermal energy and tallow production.

Thermal energy production composes almost 80% of the total AP of the tallow production chain and approximately 10% of the total EP of tallow production. As previously men- tioned, the thermal energy in biodiesel and tallow production is from the burning of heavy fuel oil, a fossil fuel with a high sulfur content. The beef and tallow production processes com- pose approximately 70% of the total EP and almost 20% of the

total AP in tallow production. In this case, the EP and AP both come from cattle management in the pasture. All urine and manure from cattle are disposed of on soil, and urine has high amounts of nitrogenous compounds such as urea that can ei- ther volatilize as ammonia or be leached by rainwater, causing local eutrophication (IPCC 2006). Ammonia also has the ca- pacity to increase soil acidity, also contributing to the AP. Figure 5 shows the total relative contribution to the GWP of each step in the tallow production process. Almost 45% of the carbon flow in tallow production is from uptake by pastures. This carbon is from the atmospheric CO2 used by plants for photosynthesis. Approximately 37% of the total carbon flow is associated with CO2 emissions from boilers that generate steam emitted directly into the atmosphere. Methane is the main flow associated with the GWP of tallow production. During cattle management, there is no control over either en- teric fermentation by cattle or manure decomposition, which increases the GWP because methane is 25 times more potent than carbon dioxide in terms of the GWP (each kg of CH4 is equivalent to 25 kg of CO2) and because cattle emit methane throughout their lifetime. Some studies (IPCC 2006; Cederberg et al. 2009) have revealed that beef cattle emit approximately 55 kg of CH4 into the atmosphere per animal every year. The main source of GWP in tallow production is methane emissions.

The GWP requires attention because the management of carbon uptake by plants in agricultural systems must be con- sidered; therefore, emissions are not the only factor to account for in the GWP. The cattle management phase encompasses both carbon emissions and uptake. Carbon uptake by the pas- ture is the main carbon uptake flow in tallow production. Carbon sequestration also occurs during cattle management and ration production. Table 3 shows how much each process
Fig. 4 Relative contributions to the AP and EP of the tallow production processes
Fig. 5 Relative contributions to the total carbon flow of each of the tallow production processes

contributes to the total carbon sequestration. Approximately 91% of all carbon uptake is due to carbon incorporation by the pasture because large areas are required for livestock feed. This number also accounts for the rotation of pastures to allow time for vegetation to recover. The remaining carbon uptake is due to the carbon incorporation into the corn and soybeans present in the rations, as mentioned above.

Table 3 also shows the contribution of each process to the total carbon emissions from tallow production. Two out of five pro- cesses produce almost 98% of the total CO2 emissions: thermal energy production and tallow production. Thermal energy pro- duction is responsible for 25% of the total carbon emissions. The main cause is heavy fuel oil burning, which releases large amounts of CO2. Tallow production is responsible for 71% of the total carbon emissions, of which the main cause is the large release of methane by livestock enteric fermentation. Ultimately, for each MJ of beef tallow biodiesel at the gate of the industry, 4.6 g of CO2 are released to the atmosphere.

Beyond the CML 2001 (April 2013) method, two other LCIA methods were used to evaluate tallow biodiesel production. The TRACI 2.1 and ReCiPe 1.08 Midpoint methods were used to compare the results obtained using the CML 2001 (Apr. 2013) methodology. The ReCiPe 1.08 and TRACI 2.1 methodologies produced the same results regarding the GWP, since they are based on IPCC emission factors (EFs) (Baitz et al. 2013). Compared to CML 2001 (April 2013), these methodologies pro- duced similar results, differing in some units but still describing the same tendency of the environmental impacts observed by the CML 2001 method. Regarding the AP, the relative contribution of tallow production to the overall impact was 99.3% based on ReCiPe 1.08 and 99% based on TRACI These results were similar to those obtained using CML 2001 (April 2013),

indicating that any of these methods can yield the same outcome. The tallow production contribution to the EP was 98.88% for the TRACI 2.1 method, again highlighting the fact that the methods presented very similar results.

The AP associated with trans- portation was due to the diesel sulfur content, which was 1800 ppm S (currently, the sulfur contents of diesel are 10

and 500 ppm S), according to the National Petroleum Agency (ANP) resolution (ANP 2013). The EP is associated with NO emissions during diesel consumption. These gases can react with water to form nitrogenous compounds, thereby contributing to environmental eutrophication and increasing the amount of nutrients present in soils. Based on the evalua- tion of the GWP of biodiesel production, tallow production, and transportation, a different pattern can be observed in Fig. 2. Therefore, tallow production has a higher environmental impact potential than biodiesel production.

Figure 3 shows the AP, EP, and GWP contributions relative to each process of biodiesel production. The "Rest of processes" is defined as all other processes that do not have significant envi- ronmental burdens alone but become significant when consid- ered together. The AP is given in sulfur dioxide equivalents (SO2-Eq.) and is associated with the ability of a substance to release H+ ions and thereby cause a local pH decrease (Baitz et al. 2013). Acidification due to biodiesel production is mainly caused by fossil fuels with high sulfur contents. In electricity production, SOX emissions from diesel burning in thermoelectric plants contribute to the AP. Fossil fuel production has a high AP because of the diesel desulfurization process and the burning of fossil fuels in refineries. In addition to electricity production, heavy fuel oil is burned in boilers to generate sufficient thermal energy to heat the reactor where the transesterification reaction occurs. This heavy fuel oil contains approximately 1% by mass of sulfur. When the oil is burned, SOx is emitted and contributes to the AP.
The EP considers the enrichment of nutrients at a certain location and is highly associated with nitrogenous compounds,
Fig. 2 Relative contributions to the impact categories of the main processes in beef tallow biodiesel production

which can cause over-fertilization of soil and water. The EP is calculated in phosphate equivalents (PO4-Eq) (Baitz et al. 2013; Wang and Mu 2014). Emissions of nitrogenous compound are higher during the treatment of effluent, which contains hydrocar- bons and organic compounds. Hydrocarbons are released into the effluent during the biodiesel wash step to remove fatty acid esters. Other organic compounds are related to the effluent treat- ment process because they are added to improve the transesterification reaction. All other processes, mostly associat- ed with the gases emitted during diesel and heavy fuel oil burn- ing, contribute less than 10% to the EP.

The key greenhouse gases (GHGs) emitted from anthropo- genic activities are carbon dioxide and methane. These gases contribute to the earth's greenhouse effect, which results in a warming effect on the planet's surface. The GWP is calculated and presented in carbon dioxide equivalents (CO2-Eq.) (Baitz et al. 2013). During biodiesel production, the GWP is signif- icant during both thermal energy production and fossil fuel production. All heavy fuel oil burned in boilers turns into CO and CO2, which enter the atmosphere. Heavy fuel oil consumption is high to fulfill the plant's thermal energy needs. Fossil fuel production also contributed to GHG emissions. In
Fig. 3 Relative contributions to the AP, EP, and GWP of the biodiesel production processes

this process, the main source of the GWP is the CO2 emitted from the burning of hydrocarbons to refine fuels (in this case, diesel and heavy fuel oil). All impact potentials related to other processes ("rest of processes") are mainly due to the production of supplies used in the biodiesel plant.

Figure 4 shows the relative contributions to the AP and EP of each process in tallow production. Fertilizer production does not have a significant impact on either of the categories evaluated because fertilizer is used in very small amounts in the pasture, reducing its entire impact chain. Another reason for the low contribution of fertilizer production in both poten- tial impact categories is that all other processes have higher emission levels than the fertilizer production process, making their relative contributions very small. Animal feed produc- tion encompasses the production of rations, which are given to cattle days before slaughter. Rations are composed of corn, soybean, urea, ammonium sulfate, and limestone. Therefore, the EP originates from the fertilizer used on corn and soybean crops and from ammonium sulfate production because all ra- tions eventually become manure that is disposed of in the field. The AP and EP from electricity production represent almost nothing with respect to thermal energy and tallow pro- duction mainly because the amount of electricity used is small, thereby reducing its impact chain and increasing the impacts associated with other processes such as thermal energy and tallow production.

Thermal energy production composes almost 80% of the total AP of the tallow production chain and approximately 10% of the total EP of tallow production. As previously men- tioned, the thermal energy in biodiesel and tallow production is from the burning of heavy fuel oil, a fossil fuel with a high sulfur content. The beef and tallow production processes com- pose approximately 70% of the total EP and almost 20% of the

total AP in tallow production. In this case, the EP and AP both come from cattle management in the pasture. All urine and manure from cattle are disposed of on soil, and urine has high amounts of nitrogenous compounds such as urea that can ei- ther volatilize as ammonia or be leached by rainwater, causing local eutrophication (IPCC 2006). Ammonia also has the ca- pacity to increase soil acidity, also contributing to the AP. Figure 5 shows the total relative contribution to the GWP of each step in the tallow production process. Almost 45% of the carbon flow in tallow production is from uptake by pastures. This carbon is from the atmospheric CO2 used by plants for photosynthesis. Approximately 37% of the total carbon flow is associated with CO2 emissions from boilers that generate steam emitted directly into the atmosphere. Methane is the main flow associated with the GWP of tallow production. During cattle management, there is no control over either en- teric fermentation by cattle or manure decomposition, which increases the GWP because methane is 25 times more potent than carbon dioxide in terms of the GWP (each kg of CH4 is equivalent to 25 kg of CO2) and because cattle emit methane throughout their lifetime. Some studies (IPCC 2006; Cederberg et al. 2009) have revealed that beef cattle emit approximately 55 kg of CH4 into the atmosphere per animal every year. The main source of GWP in tallow production is methane emissions.

The GWP requires attention because the management of carbon uptake by plants in agricultural systems must be con- sidered; therefore, emissions are not the only factor to account for in the GWP. The cattle management phase encompasses both carbon emissions and uptake. Carbon uptake by the pas- ture is the main carbon uptake flow in tallow production. Carbon sequestration also occurs during cattle management and ration production. Table 3 shows how much each process
Fig. 4 Relative contributions to the AP and EP of the tallow production processes
Fig. 5 Relative contributions to the total carbon flow of each of the tallow production processes

contributes to the total carbon sequestration. Approximately 91% of all carbon uptake is due to carbon incorporation by the pasture because large areas are required for livestock feed. This number also accounts for the rotation of pastures to allow time for vegetation to recover. The remaining carbon uptake is due to the carbon incorporation into the corn and soybeans present in the rations, as mentioned above.

Table 3 also shows the contribution of each process to the total carbon emissions from tallow production. Two out of five pro- cesses produce almost 98% of the total CO2 emissions: thermal energy production and tallow production. Thermal energy pro- duction is responsible for 25% of the total carbon emissions. The main cause is heavy fuel oil burning, which releases large amounts of CO2. Tallow production is responsible for 71% of the total carbon emissions, of which the main cause is the large release of methane by livestock enteric fermentation. Ultimately, for each MJ of beef tallow biodiesel at the gate of the industry, 4.6 g of CO2 are released to the atmosphere.

Beyond the CML 2001 (April 2013) method, two other LCIA methods were used to evaluate tallow biodiesel production. The TRACI 2.1 and ReCiPe 1.08 Midpoint methods were used to compare the results obtained using the CML 2001 (Apr. 2013) methodology. The ReCiPe 1.08 and TRACI 2.1 methodologies produced the same results regarding the GWP, since they are based on IPCC emission factors (EFs) (Baitz et al. 2013). Compared to CML 2001 (April 2013), these methodologies pro- duced similar results, differing in some units but still describing the same tendency of the environmental impacts observed by the CML 2001 method. Regarding the AP, the relative contribution of tallow production to the overall impact was 99.3% based on ReCiPe 1.08 and 99% based on TRACI These results were similar to those obtained using CML 2001 (April 2013),

indicating that any of these methods can yield the same outcome. The tallow production contribution to the EP was 98.88% for the TRACI 2.1 method, again highlighting the fact that the methods presented very similar results.

All urine and manure from cattle are disposed of on soil, and urine has high amounts of nitrogenous compounds such as urea that can ei- ther volatilize as ammonia or be leached by rainwater, causing local eutrophication (IPCC 2006). Ammonia also has the ca- pacity to increase soil acidity, also contributing to the AP. Figure 5 shows the total relative contribution to the GWP of each step in the tallow production process. Almost 45% of the carbon flow in tallow production is from uptake by pastures. This carbon is from the atmospheric CO2 used by plants for photosynthesis. Approximately 37% of the total carbon flow is associated with CO2 emissions from boilers that generate steam emitted directly into the atmosphere. Methane is the main flow associated with the GWP of tallow production. During cattle management, there is no control over either en- teric fermentation by cattle or manure decomposition, which increases the GWP because methane is 25 times more potent than carbon dioxide in terms of the GWP (each kg of CH4 is equivalent to 25 kg of CO2) and because cattle emit methane throughout their lifetime. Some studies (IPCC 2006; Cederberg et al. 2009) have revealed that beef cattle emit approximately 55 kg of CH4 into the atmosphere per animal every year. The main source of GWP in tallow production is methane emissions.

The GWP requires attention because the management of carbon uptake by plants in agricultural systems must be con- sidered; therefore, emissions are not the only factor to account for in the GWP. The cattle management phase encompasses both carbon emissions and uptake. Carbon uptake by the pas- ture is the main carbon uptake flow in tallow production. Carbon sequestration also occurs during cattle management and ration production. Table 3 shows how much each process
Fig. 4 Relative contributions to the AP and EP of the tallow production processes
Fig. 5 Relative contributions to the total carbon flow of each of the tallow production processes

contributes to the total carbon sequestration. Approximately 91% of all carbon uptake is due to carbon incorporation by the pasture because large areas are required for livestock feed. This number also accounts for the rotation of pastures to allow time for vegetation to recover. The remaining carbon uptake is due to the carbon incorporation into the corn and soybeans present in the rations, as mentioned above.

Table 3 also shows the contribution of each process to the total carbon emissions from tallow production. Two out of five pro- cesses produce almost 98% of the total CO2 emissions: thermal energy production and tallow production. Thermal energy pro- duction is responsible for 25% of the total carbon emissions. The main cause is heavy fuel oil burning, which releases large amounts of CO2. Tallow production is responsible for 71% of the total carbon emissions, of which the main cause is the large release of methane by livestock enteric fermentation. Ultimately, for each MJ of beef tallow biodiesel at the gate of the industry, 4.6 g of CO2 are released to the atmosphere.

Beyond the CML 2001 (April 2013) method, two other LCIA methods were used to evaluate tallow biodiesel production. The TRACI 2.1 and ReCiPe 1.08 Midpoint methods were used to compare the results obtained using the CML 2001 (Apr. 2013) methodology. The ReCiPe 1.08 and TRACI 2.1 methodologies produced the same results regarding the GWP, since they are based on IPCC emission factors (EFs) (Baitz et al. 2013). Compared to CML 2001 (April 2013), these methodologies pro- duced similar results, differing in some units but still describing the same tendency of the environmental impacts observed by the CML 2001 method. Regarding the AP, the relative contribution of tallow production to the overall impact was 99.3% based on ReCiPe 1.08 and 99% based on TRACI These results were similar to those obtained using CML 2001 (April 2013),

indicating that any of these methods can yield the same outcome. The tallow production contribution to the EP was 98.88% for the TRACI 2.1 method, again highlighting the fact that the methods presented very similar results.
43: <header>3.2 Identification of the main sources of environmental impacts </header>

The relative environmental impact results shown in Fig. 2 represent the contribution of each major process to the total emissions. Tallow transportation was accounted for because the modeled system included tallow transportation to the bio- diesel plant over a significant distance.

The AP and EP showed the same pattern (major contribu- tions to environmental impacts of beef tallow production), although a different pattern was identified for the GWP (bal- anced contributions to biodiesel and tallow production). In both cases, tallow transportation had a minimal contribution to all impact categories. Concerning diesel, low consumption based on distance was assumed. The AP associated with trans- portation was due to the diesel sulfur content, which was 1800 ppm S (currently, the sulfur contents of diesel are 10

and 500 ppm S), according to the National Petroleum Agency (ANP) resolution (ANP 2013). The EP is associated with NO emissions during diesel consumption. These gases can react with water to form nitrogenous compounds, thereby contributing to environmental eutrophication and increasing the amount of nutrients present in soils. Based on the evalua- tion of the GWP of biodiesel production, tallow production, and transportation, a different pattern can be observed in Fig. 2. Therefore, tallow production has a higher environmental impact potential than biodiesel production.

Figure 3 shows the AP, EP, and GWP contributions relative to each process of biodiesel production. The "Rest of processes" is defined as all other processes that do not have significant envi- ronmental burdens alone but become significant when consid- ered together. The AP is given in sulfur dioxide equivalents (SO2-Eq.) and is associated with the ability of a substance to release H+ ions and thereby cause a local pH decrease (Baitz et al. 2013). Acidification due to biodiesel production is mainly caused by fossil fuels with high sulfur contents. In electricity production, SOX emissions from diesel burning in thermoelectric plants contribute to the AP. Fossil fuel production has a high AP because of the diesel desulfurization process and the burning of fossil fuels in refineries. In addition to electricity production, heavy fuel oil is burned in boilers to generate sufficient thermal energy to heat the reactor where the transesterification reaction occurs. This heavy fuel oil contains approximately 1% by mass of sulfur. When the oil is burned, SOx is emitted and contributes to the AP.
The EP considers the enrichment of nutrients at a certain location and is highly associated with nitrogenous compounds,
Fig. 2 Relative contributions to the impact categories of the main processes in beef tallow biodiesel production

which can cause over-fertilization of soil and water. The EP is calculated in phosphate equivalents (PO4-Eq) (Baitz et al. 2013; Wang and Mu 2014). Emissions of nitrogenous compound are higher during the treatment of effluent, which contains hydrocar- bons and organic compounds. Hydrocarbons are released into the effluent during the biodiesel wash step to remove fatty acid esters. Other organic compounds are related to the effluent treat- ment process because they are added to improve the transesterification reaction. All other processes, mostly associat- ed with the gases emitted during diesel and heavy fuel oil burn- ing, contribute less than 10% to the EP.

The key greenhouse gases (GHGs) emitted from anthropo- genic activities are carbon dioxide and methane. These gases contribute to the earth's greenhouse effect, which results in a warming effect on the planet's surface. The GWP is calculated and presented in carbon dioxide equivalents (CO2-Eq.) (Baitz et al. 2013). During biodiesel production, the GWP is signif- icant during both thermal energy production and fossil fuel production. All heavy fuel oil burned in boilers turns into CO and CO2, which enter the atmosphere. Heavy fuel oil consumption is high to fulfill the plant's thermal energy needs. Fossil fuel production also contributed to GHG emissions. In
Fig. 3 Relative contributions to the AP, EP, and GWP of the biodiesel production processes

this process, the main source of the GWP is the CO2 emitted from the burning of hydrocarbons to refine fuels (in this case, diesel and heavy fuel oil). All impact potentials related to other processes ("rest of processes") are mainly due to the production of supplies used in the biodiesel plant.

Figure 4 shows the relative contributions to the AP and EP of each process in tallow production. Fertilizer production does not have a significant impact on either of the categories evaluated because fertilizer is used in very small amounts in the pasture, reducing its entire impact chain. Another reason for the low contribution of fertilizer production in both poten- tial impact categories is that all other processes have higher emission levels than the fertilizer production process, making their relative contributions very small. Animal feed produc- tion encompasses the production of rations, which are given to cattle days before slaughter. Rations are composed of corn, soybean, urea, ammonium sulfate, and limestone. Therefore, the EP originates from the fertilizer used on corn and soybean crops and from ammonium sulfate production because all ra- tions eventually become manure that is disposed of in the field. The AP and EP from electricity production represent almost nothing with respect to thermal energy and tallow pro- duction mainly because the amount of electricity used is small, thereby reducing its impact chain and increasing the impacts associated with other processes such as thermal energy and tallow production.

Thermal energy production composes almost 80% of the total AP of the tallow production chain and approximately 10% of the total EP of tallow production.
