Figure 3 shows the AP, EP, and GWP contributions relative to each process of biodiesel production. The "Rest of processes" is defined as all other processes that do not have significant envi- ronmental burdens alone but become significant when consid- ered together. The AP is given in sulfur dioxide equivalents (SO2-Eq.) and is associated with the ability of a substance to release H+ ions and thereby cause a local pH decrease (Baitz et al. 2013). Acidification due to biodiesel production is mainly caused by fossil fuels with high sulfur contents. In electricity production, SOX emissions from diesel burning in thermoelectric plants contribute to the AP. Fossil fuel production has a high AP because of the diesel desulfurization process and the burning of fossil fuels in refineries. In addition to electricity production, heavy fuel oil is burned in boilers to generate sufficient thermal energy to heat the reactor where the transesterification reaction occurs. This heavy fuel oil contains approximately 1% by mass of sulfur. When the oil is burned, SOx is emitted and contributes to the AP.
The EP considers the enrichment of nutrients at a certain location and is highly associated with nitrogenous compounds,
Fig. 2 Relative contributions to the impact categories of the main processes in beef tallow biodiesel production

which can cause over-fertilization of soil and water. The EP is calculated in phosphate equivalents (PO4-Eq) (Baitz et al. 2013; Wang and Mu 2014). Emissions of nitrogenous compound are higher during the treatment of effluent, which contains hydrocar- bons and organic compounds. Hydrocarbons are released into the effluent during the biodiesel wash step to remove fatty acid esters. Other organic compounds are related to the effluent treat- ment process because they are added to improve the transesterification reaction. All other processes, mostly associat- ed with the gases emitted during diesel and heavy fuel oil burn- ing, contribute less than 10% to the EP.

The key greenhouse gases (GHGs) emitted from anthropo- genic activities are carbon dioxide and methane. These gases contribute to the earth's greenhouse effect, which results in a warming effect on the planet's surface. The GWP is calculated and presented in carbon dioxide equivalents (CO2-Eq.) (Baitz et al. 2013). During biodiesel production, the GWP is signif- icant during both thermal energy production and fossil fuel production. All heavy fuel oil burned in boilers turns into CO and CO2, which enter the atmosphere. Heavy fuel oil consumption is high to fulfill the plant's thermal energy needs. Fossil fuel production also contributed to GHG emissions. In
Fig. 3 Relative contributions to the AP, EP, and GWP of the biodiesel production processes

this process, the main source of the GWP is the CO2 emitted from the burning of hydrocarbons to refine fuels (in this case, diesel and heavy fuel oil). All impact potentials related to other processes ("rest of processes") are mainly due to the production of supplies used in the biodiesel plant.

Figure 4 shows the relative contributions to the AP and EP of each process in tallow production. Fertilizer production does not have a significant impact on either of the categories evaluated because fertilizer is used in very small amounts in the pasture, reducing its entire impact chain. Another reason for the low contribution of fertilizer production in both poten- tial impact categories is that all other processes have higher emission levels than the fertilizer production process, making their relative contributions very small. Animal feed produc- tion encompasses the production of rations, which are given to cattle days before slaughter. Rations are composed of corn, soybean, urea, ammonium sulfate, and limestone. Therefore, the EP originates from the fertilizer used on corn and soybean crops and from ammonium sulfate production because all ra- tions eventually become manure that is disposed of in the field. The AP and EP from electricity production represent almost nothing with respect to thermal energy and tallow pro- duction mainly because the amount of electricity used is small, thereby reducing its impact chain and increasing the impacts associated with other processes such as thermal energy and tallow production.

Thermal energy production composes almost 80% of the total AP of the tallow production chain and approximately 10% of the total EP of tallow production. As previously men- tioned, the thermal energy in biodiesel and tallow production is from the burning of heavy fuel oil, a fossil fuel with a high sulfur content. The beef and tallow production processes com- pose approximately 70% of the total EP and almost 20% of the

total AP in tallow production. In this case, the EP and AP both come from cattle management in the pasture. All urine and manure from cattle are disposed of on soil, and urine has high amounts of nitrogenous compounds such as urea that can ei- ther volatilize as ammonia or be leached by rainwater, causing local eutrophication (IPCC 2006). Ammonia also has the ca- pacity to increase soil acidity, also contributing to the AP. Figure 5 shows the total relative contribution to the GWP of each step in the tallow production process. Almost 45% of the carbon flow in tallow production is from uptake by pastures. This carbon is from the atmospheric CO2 used by plants for photosynthesis. Approximately 37% of the total carbon flow is associated with CO2 emissions from boilers that generate steam emitted directly into the atmosphere. Methane is the main flow associated with the GWP of tallow production. During cattle management, there is no control over either en- teric fermentation by cattle or manure decomposition, which increases the GWP because methane is 25 times more potent than carbon dioxide in terms of the GWP (each kg of CH4 is equivalent to 25 kg of CO2) and because cattle emit methane throughout their lifetime. Some studies (IPCC 2006; Cederberg et al. 2009) have revealed that beef cattle emit approximately 55 kg of CH4 into the atmosphere per animal every year. The main source of GWP in tallow production is methane emissions.

The GWP requires attention because the management of carbon uptake by plants in agricultural systems must be con- sidered; therefore, emissions are not the only factor to account for in the GWP. The cattle management phase encompasses both carbon emissions and uptake. Carbon uptake by the pas- ture is the main carbon uptake flow in tallow production. Carbon sequestration also occurs during cattle management and ration production. Table 3 shows how much each process
Fig. 4 Relative contributions to the AP and EP of the tallow production processes
Fig. 5 Relative contributions to the total carbon flow of each of the tallow production processes

contributes to the total carbon sequestration. Approximately 91% of all carbon uptake is due to carbon incorporation by the pasture because large areas are required for livestock feed. This number also accounts for the rotation of pastures to allow time for vegetation to recover. The remaining carbon uptake is due to the carbon incorporation into the corn and soybeans present in the rations, as mentioned above.

Table 3 also shows the contribution of each process to the total carbon emissions from tallow production. Two out of five pro- cesses produce almost 98% of the total CO2 emissions: thermal energy production and tallow production. Thermal energy pro- duction is responsible for 25% of the total carbon emissions. The main cause is heavy fuel oil burning, which releases large amounts of CO2. Tallow production is responsible for 71% of the total carbon emissions, of which the main cause is the large release of methane by livestock enteric fermentation. Ultimately, for each MJ of beef tallow biodiesel at the gate of the industry, 4.6 g of CO2 are released to the atmosphere.

Beyond the CML 2001 (April 2013) method, two other LCIA methods were used to evaluate tallow biodiesel production. The TRACI 2.1 and ReCiPe 1.08 Midpoint methods were used to compare the results obtained using the CML 2001 (Apr. 2013) methodology. The ReCiPe 1.08 and TRACI 2.1 methodologies produced the same results regarding the GWP, since they are based on IPCC emission factors (EFs) (Baitz et al. 2013). Compared to CML 2001 (April 2013), these methodologies pro- duced similar results, differing in some units but still describing the same tendency of the environmental impacts observed by the CML 2001 method. Regarding the AP, the relative contribution of tallow production to the overall impact was 99.3% based on ReCiPe 1.08 and 99% based on TRACI These results were similar to those obtained using CML 2001 (April 2013),

indicating that any of these methods can yield the same outcome. The tallow production contribution to the EP was 98.88% for the TRACI 2.1 method, again highlighting the fact that the methods presented very similar results.
47: Three scenarios were modeled to determine the impacts of changing parameters and of inputs to the raw materials and fuel production:

<list>S1: Uncertainty in the EFs; 
S2: A 10% variation in electrical and thermal energy consumption; 
S3: Change in boneless meat and tallow prices.</list>
The results, presented in Fig. 6, are compared with the reference case.
Table 3 Relative contributions to the GWP of each process in tallow production, divided into carbon uptake and emissions

|Process|% Contribution Uptake|Animal feed production|8.699 Uptake|Carbon uptake by pasture|91.301 Emission|Fertilizer production|0.180 Emission|Electricity production|1.868 Emission|Thermal energy production|25.674 Emission|Tallow production|71.714 Emission|Other processes|0.564

The environmental impact of the system is mainly related to the raw material phase and, consequently, variation in the EFs. The sensitivity analysis shows that an increase of 30% in the EF while maintaining the same inputs (S1) will increase the GWP by 350.43% (Fig. 6a), the EP by 11.24%, and the AP by 5.17%. The BWC and BWU indicators are not affected by this variation because the EF only changes the amount of gases that are released to the atmosphere. In other words, the EF uncertainty can sharply change the LCA results. Since no change in carbon uptake was considered, the variations in the GWP were due mainly to enteric fermentation emissions. Methane emissions from enteric fermentation are difficult to estimate because there are many cattle breeds under different conditions. In addition, the nitrous oxide EF may vary widely, which makes accurately accounting for this factor difficult, as in the case of methane. The AP and EP variations are in the 15% range because the emissions that cause the AP and EP
were relatively low compared to those that cause the GWP. The EFs associated with the AP and EP are lower than the EFs associated with the GWP, resulting in lower environmental impact variation.

Energy consumption is a key parameter of tallow and biodiesel production. Both require high quantities of ther- mal energy for heating boilers to render feedstock in the cooking or heating lines where transesterification occurs. Electricity is also required for the maintenance of both processes, as well as in auxiliary processes. Figure 6b shows how an energy consumption variation of 10% (S2) changes the environmental profile of tallow biodie- sel. The sensitivity analysis reveals increases of approxi- mately 44.86% in the GWP, 0.84% in the AP, 7.94% in the EP, 8.31% in the BWU indicator, and 3.18% in the BWC indicator when 10% more thermal energy and elec- tricity are needed. When more energy is needed, more
Fig. 6 Sensitivity analysis results for the GWP in a S1, b S2, and c S3

fuels are used. Thermal energy in this case depends ex- clusively on heavy fuel oil from fossil sources. Therefore, higher CO2 quantities will be released to the atmosphere, contributing to the increase in the GWP. However, the major source of electricity in Brazil is hydraulic (BEN 2014). The contribution of electricity to the increased GWP is much smaller than the thermal energy input per year. Thus, when increasing or decreasing the energy con- sumption, thermal energy production is responsible for the GWP variation. The AP and its increases are due to the consumption of heavy fuel oil, which has a sulfur content of 1%. The BWU indicator is higher in this scenario be- cause more fuel was needed to meet the new thermal energy demand and more blue water is needed to produce the thermal energy. The BWC indicator is also higher but not to the same degree as the BWU indicator. Again, blue water is needed, but the BWC indicator accounts for the degradative use of water in addition to that accounted for by the BWU indicator. Therefore, the BWC indicator is higher in this scenario because the amount of water need- ed is greater and portions of this water, such as vapor to the atmosphere, cooling water, and wastewater, differ in terms of quality.

The beef market is well established throughout the world (Makiya and Fraisse 2015). In this case, beef and tallow prices are key parameters because price allocation is used to correct- ly allocate environmental impacts according to their market price. Beef and tallow prices are very different. Beef is the main product of raising cattle, whereas tallow, a by-product, has a much lower price. In Brazil, the average beef price in 2015 was $4.61 per kilogram, while the average tallow price was six times less, namely $0.74 per kilogram. In fact, most of the impact is attributed to beef, and only a small amount of the impact is attributed to tallow. As mentioned above, economic allocation is important in this regard to eliminate the environ- mental burden of a product. To evaluate fluctuations in beef and tallow prices, the price of beef was assessed at I 20% of the original price (S3). The beef price is more resilient to change than the beef tallow price, as beef has a higher price and is used exclusively for food, unlike tallow, which is ob- tained as a by-product and used in industries such as soap and biodiesel. Looking at Fig. 6c, we can clearly observe that the price change directly influences the environmental impacts when economically allocated. With an increase of 20% in the price of beef, the GWP decreases by 283.64%. When the difference in price between tallow and beef is large, the GWP tends to decrease because the economic allocation will attri- bute more environmental impact to beef than tallow. When the price of beef is 20% lower, there is an increase of 311.55% in the GWP. This variation was expected because when tallow and beef prices are similar, economic allocation tends to attri- bute more burden to tallow, which has a lower price. The same pattern was observed when the AP, EP, and BWU and BWC
indicators were evaluated. When the price of beef is higher, the AP, EP, BWU, and BWC decrease by approximately 19%, and when the price is lower, they all increase by approximate- ly 18%.

In other words, the EF uncertainty can sharply change the LCA results. Since no change in carbon uptake was considered, the variations in the GWP were due mainly to enteric fermentation emissions. Methane emissions from enteric fermentation are difficult to estimate because there are many cattle breeds under different conditions. In addition, the nitrous oxide EF may vary widely, which makes accurately accounting for this factor difficult, as in the case of methane. The AP and EP variations are in the 15% range because the emissions that cause the AP and EP
were relatively low compared to those that cause the GWP. The EFs associated with the AP and EP are lower than the EFs associated with the GWP, resulting in lower environmental impact variation.

Energy consumption is a key parameter of tallow and biodiesel production. Both require high quantities of ther- mal energy for heating boilers to render feedstock in the cooking or heating lines where transesterification occurs. Electricity is also required for the maintenance of both processes, as well as in auxiliary processes. Figure 6b shows how an energy consumption variation of 10% (S2) changes the environmental profile of tallow biodie- sel. The sensitivity analysis reveals increases of approxi- mately 44.86% in the GWP, 0.84% in the AP, 7.94% in the EP, 8.31% in the BWU indicator, and 3.18% in the BWC indicator when 10% more thermal energy and elec- tricity are needed. When more energy is needed, more
Fig. 6 Sensitivity analysis results for the GWP in a S1, b S2, and c S3

fuels are used. Thermal energy in this case depends ex- clusively on heavy fuel oil from fossil sources. Therefore, higher CO2 quantities will be released to the atmosphere, contributing to the increase in the GWP. However, the major source of electricity in Brazil is hydraulic (BEN 2014). The contribution of electricity to the increased GWP is much smaller than the thermal energy input per year. Thus, when increasing or decreasing the energy con- sumption, thermal energy production is responsible for the GWP variation. The AP and its increases are due to the consumption of heavy fuel oil, which has a sulfur content of 1%. The BWU indicator is higher in this scenario be- cause more fuel was needed to meet the new thermal energy demand and more blue water is needed to produce the thermal energy. The BWC indicator is also higher but not to the same degree as the BWU indicator. Again, blue water is needed, but the BWC indicator accounts for the degradative use of water in addition to that accounted for by the BWU indicator. Therefore, the BWC indicator is higher in this scenario because the amount of water need- ed is greater and portions of this water, such as vapor to the atmosphere, cooling water, and wastewater, differ in terms of quality.

The beef market is well established throughout the world (Makiya and Fraisse 2015). In this case, beef and tallow prices are key parameters because price allocation is used to correct- ly allocate environmental impacts according to their market price. Beef and tallow prices are very different. Beef is the main product of raising cattle, whereas tallow, a by-product, has a much lower price. In Brazil, the average beef price in 2015 was $4.61 per kilogram, while the average tallow price was six times less, namely $0.74 per kilogram. In fact, most of the impact is attributed to beef, and only a small amount of the impact is attributed to tallow. As mentioned above, economic allocation is important in this regard to eliminate the environ- mental burden of a product. To evaluate fluctuations in beef and tallow prices, the price of beef was assessed at I 20% of the original price (S3). The beef price is more resilient to change than the beef tallow price, as beef has a higher price and is used exclusively for food, unlike tallow, which is ob- tained as a by-product and used in industries such as soap and biodiesel. Looking at Fig. 6c, we can clearly observe that the price change directly influences the environmental impacts when economically allocated. With an increase of 20% in the price of beef, the GWP decreases by 283.64%. When the difference in price between tallow and beef is large, the GWP tends to decrease because the economic allocation will attri- bute more environmental impact to beef than tallow. When the price of beef is 20% lower, there is an increase of 311.55% in the GWP. This variation was expected because when tallow and beef prices are similar, economic allocation tends to attri- bute more burden to tallow, which has a lower price. The same pattern was observed when the AP, EP, and BWU and BWC
indicators were evaluated. When the price of beef is higher, the AP, EP, BWU, and BWC decrease by approximately 19%, and when the price is lower, they all increase by approximate- ly 18%.

<list>S1: Uncertainty in the EFs; 
S2: A 10% variation in electrical and thermal energy consumption; 
S3: Change in boneless meat and tallow prices.</list>
The results, presented in Fig. 6, are compared with the reference case.

54: This study presented an LCIA of the beef tallow biodiesel system in Brazil, which was defined from a cradle-to-gate perspective. Considering Brazil's potential to produce larger quantities of biodiesel and the obligation to replace diesel with biodiesel according to current legislation (Presidência da República 2016), this work constitutes a baseline for analyz- ing the environmental impacts of tallow production and country-specific pathways.

Primary data allowed LCI characterization and assessment of the LCI of beef tallow biodiesel production. Carbon flux results indicate that beef tallow biodiesel production acts as a carbon source. Namely, pasture carbon uptake (91% of all uptake) is smaller than combined biogenic and fossil CO2 emissions, which are controlled by cattle enteric fermentation as methane (72%) and by thermal energy processes (25%). Otherwise, thermal energy production accounts for 80% of total AP emissions, and cattle urine and manure are responsi- ble for 70% of total EP emissions.

The BWC and BWU indicators, used to assess the water footprint of the whole process, are controlled by electricity usage, which is greater than 90% for each indicator due to the 70% hydropower share of total Brazilian energy.

The sensitivity analysis of the influence of EFs associated with enteric fermentation showed that an error of + 30% could represent an increase of up to 350% in GWP. In addition, the economic allocation sensitivity analysis demonstrated that the ratio between beef and tallow prices strongly influences all environmental impacts. Specifically, a variation of 20% of the original beef price results in a change of - 283.64% to + 311.55% to total GWP.

Primary data allowed LCI characterization and assessment of the LCI of beef tallow biodiesel production. Carbon flux results indicate that beef tallow biodiesel production acts as a carbon source. Namely, pasture carbon uptake (91% of all uptake) is smaller than combined biogenic and fossil CO2 emissions, which are controlled by cattle enteric fermentation as methane (72%) and by thermal energy processes (25%). Otherwise, thermal energy production accounts for 80% of total AP emissions, and cattle urine and manure are responsi- ble for 70% of total EP emissions.

The BWC and BWU indicators, used to assess the water footprint of the whole process, are controlled by electricity usage, which is greater than 90% for each indicator due to the 70% hydropower share of total Brazilian energy.

56: Carbon and nitrogen cycles within pastures should be im- proved by using primary data from grazing sites, allowing a more accurate assessment of biodiesel production from tallow. This information should allow a more accurate determination of EFs, primarily carbon uptake, which will directly affect the LCI results. Moreover, primary data on cattle management methods should be obtained to expand the LCA study. Although cattle management in Brazil is primarily extensive, intensive and semi-intensive management techniques should also be assessed to build a more robust model of cattle pro- duction in Brazil. Due to the lack of data about water

58: Carbon and nitrogen cycles within pastures should be im- proved by using primary data from grazing sites, allowing a more accurate assessment of biodiesel production from tallow. This information should allow a more accurate determination of EFs, primarily carbon uptake, which will directly affect the LCI results. Moreover, primary data on cattle management methods should be obtained to expand the LCA study. Although cattle management in Brazil is primarily extensive, intensive and semi-intensive management techniques should also be assessed to build a more robust model of cattle pro- duction in Brazil. 60: Carbon and nitrogen cycles within pastures should be im- proved by using primary data from grazing sites, allowing a more accurate assessment of biodiesel production from tallow. This information should allow a more accurate determination of EFs, primarily carbon uptake, which will directly affect the LCI results. Moreover, primary data on cattle management methods should be obtained to expand the LCA study. Although cattle management in Brazil is primarily extensive, intensive and semi-intensive management techniques should also be assessed to build a more robust model of cattle pro- duction in Brazil. Due to the lack of data about water

consumption in all productive chains, we made some assump- tions about water use and effluents in this study, as defined in|Cho HJ, Kim J, Ahmed F, Yeo T (2013) Life-cycle greenhouse gas emis- sions and energy balances of a biodiesel production from palm fatty acid distillate (PFAD). Appl Energ 111:479-488 "the ""Methods"" section. Due to these assumptions, it is impor- tant to consider primary data in further studies. Thermal ener- gy is still a bottleneck for the competitiveness of biodiesel in the fuel market even though Brazil mixes it in large quantities"|Corrêa ES, Vieira A, Costa FP, Cezar IM (2000) Sistema semi-intensivo de produção de carne de bovinos nelores no Centro-Oeste do Brasil, Campo Grande: Embrapa Gado de Corte, 49p.Available at: http:// www.infoteca.cnptia.embrapa.br/handle/doc/324073 with diesel. The change in the energy matrix related to the production of biodiesel is fundamental for increasing the com- petitiveness of this fuel, particularly since it is one of the most promising options for the future sustainability and substitution of fossil fuels. Finally, this paper advances the knowledge related to the beef tallow and tallow biodiesel supply chains in Brazil.|Costa KAP, Oliveira IP, Faquin V (2006) Adubação nitrogenada para pastagens do gênero Brachiaria em solos do Cerrado, Santo Antônio de Goiás: Embrapa Arroz e Feijão, 60p. 63: Carbon and nitrogen cycles within pastures should be im- proved by using primary data from grazing sites, allowing a more accurate assessment of biodiesel production from tallow. This information should allow a more accurate determination of EFs, primarily carbon uptake, which will directly affect the LCI results. Moreover, primary data on cattle management methods should be obtained to expand the LCA study. Although cattle management in Brazil is primarily extensive, intensive and semi-intensive management techniques should also be assessed to build a more robust model of cattle pro- duction in Brazil. Due to the lack of data about water

consumption in all productive chains, we made some assump- tions about water use and effluents in this study, as defined in|Cho HJ, Kim J, Ahmed F, Yeo T (2013) Life-cycle greenhouse gas emis- sions and energy balances of a biodiesel production from palm fatty acid distillate (PFAD). Appl Energ 111:479-488 "the ""Methods"" section. Due to these assumptions, it is impor- tant to consider primary data in further studies. Thermal ener- gy is still a bottleneck for the competitiveness of biodiesel in the fuel market even though Brazil mixes it in large quantities"|Corrêa ES, Vieira A, Costa FP, Cezar IM (2000) Sistema semi-intensivo de produção de carne de bovinos nelores no Centro-Oeste do Brasil, Campo Grande: Embrapa Gado de Corte, 49p.Available at: http:// www.infoteca.cnptia.embrapa.br/handle/doc/324073 with diesel. The change in the energy matrix related to the production of biodiesel is fundamental for increasing the com- petitiveness of this fuel, particularly since it is one of the most promising options for the future sustainability and substitution of fossil fuels. Finally, this paper advances the knowledge related to the beef tallow and tallow biodiesel supply chains in Brazil.|Costa KAP, Oliveira IP, Faquin V (2006) Adubação nitrogenada para pastagens do gênero Brachiaria em solos do Cerrado, Santo Antônio de Goiás: Embrapa Arroz e Feijão, 60p. 65: Carbon and nitrogen cycles within pastures should be im- proved by using primary data from grazing sites, allowing a more accurate assessment of biodiesel production from tallow. This information should allow a more accurate determination of EFs, primarily carbon uptake, which will directly affect the LCI results. Moreover, primary data on cattle management methods should be obtained to expand the LCA study. Although cattle management in Brazil is primarily extensive, intensive and semi-intensive management techniques should also be assessed to build a more robust model of cattle pro- duction in Brazil. Due to the lack of data about water

consumption in all productive chains, we made some assump- tions about water use and effluents in this study, as defined in|Cho HJ, Kim J, Ahmed F, Yeo T (2013) Life-cycle greenhouse gas emis- sions and energy balances of a biodiesel production from palm fatty acid distillate (PFAD). Appl Energ 111:479-488 "the ""Methods"" section. Due to these assumptions, it is impor- tant to consider primary data in further studies. Thermal ener- gy is still a bottleneck for the competitiveness of biodiesel in the fuel market even though Brazil mixes it in large quantities"|Corrêa ES, Vieira A, Costa FP, Cezar IM (2000) Sistema semi-intensivo de produção de carne de bovinos nelores no Centro-Oeste do Brasil, Campo Grande: Embrapa Gado de Corte, 49p.Available at: http:// www.infoteca.cnptia.embrapa.br/handle/doc/324073 with diesel. The change in the energy matrix related to the production of biodiesel is fundamental for increasing the com- petitiveness of this fuel, particularly since it is one of the most promising options for the future sustainability and substitution of fossil fuels.
